title: DAVID — The Language No One Wrote Down

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Proto-Indo-European — the ancestor of Sanskrit, Latin, Greek, English, Persian, and hundreds more — a language spoken around five thousand years ago that no one ever wrote down.

There is no tablet. No inscription. No manuscript. Every sound I give you today is a reconstruction — built from the comparative method, working backwards through daughter languages to find the forms they all share.
So I ask the question differently for this language: not who kept speaking it — but what do the descendants preserve about what it sounded like?

The proof is in the systematic correspondences: Sanskrit pitar, Latin pater, Greek patēr, Old English fæder — the same word for father, with the same sound shifts, in languages separated by thousands of miles and two thousand years.

We reconstruct Proto-Indo-European corpus-first — but the corpus is the daughter languages themselves: Grimm's Law governs Germanic consonant shifts, Brugmann's Law governs vowel lengthening, and Hittite laryngeals confirmed what Saussure predicted from internal reconstruction alone.

Every form I say is marked with an asterisk — the scholarly convention for reconstructed, never directly attested. The consonants are well-established; accent and intonation are debated. All of this is inference, disciplined by method, not evidence from any surviving document.
Listen — *pṓds — foot. *wódr̥ — water. *ph₂tḗr — father. Each asterisk carries two thousand years of comparative work — the closest we can come to hearing a voice that left no writing behind.
Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
